Cuban-Italian actor (1933–2017)

**Tomás Quintín Rodríguez-Varona Milián Salinas de la Fé y Álvarez de la Campa**[[1]](#cite_note-1) (March 3, 1933 – March 22, 2017) was a Cuban-born actor and musician, who worked extensively in American and Italian films. Born in [Havana](/wiki/Havana "Havana") and educated at the [Actors Studio](/wiki/Actors_Studio "Actors Studio") in New York, Milian began his acting career in the United States before moving to Italy in the late 1950s, where he became best known for the emotional intensity and humor he brought to starring roles in [genre films](/wiki/B_movie "B movie").

Throughout the late-1960s and early-1970s, Milian established himself as a dynamic leading actor in a series of [Spaghetti Western](/wiki/Spaghetti_Western "Spaghetti Western") films, most notably *[The Big Gundown](/wiki/The_Big_Gundown "The Big Gundown")* (1966), *[Django Kill... If You Live, Shoot!](/wiki/Django_Kill..._If_You_Live,_Shoot! "Django Kill... If You Live, Shoot!")* (1967),[[2]](#cite_note-Milian-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Bio-3) as well as [Sergio Corbucci](/wiki/Sergio_Corbucci "Sergio Corbucci")'s parody of the genre *[The White, the Yellow, and the Black](/wiki/The_White,_the_Yellow,_and_the_Black "The White, the Yellow, and the Black")* (1975), and [Dennis Hopper](/wiki/Dennis_Hopper "Dennis Hopper")'s Western-influenced arthouse film *[The Last Movie](/wiki/The_Last_Movie "The Last Movie")* (1971).

After the decline of Spaghetti Westerns, Milian transitioned to *[poliziottesco](/wiki/Poliziottesco "Poliziottesco")* films. He was acclaimed as a psychotic killer in *[Almost Human](/wiki/Almost_Human_(1974_film) "Almost Human (1974 film)")* (1974), and appeared in *[Emergency Squad](/wiki/Emergency_Squad_(1974_film) "Emergency Squad (1974 film)")* (1974), *[The Tough Ones](/wiki/The_Tough_Ones_(1976_film) "The Tough Ones (1976 film)")* (1976) and *[The Cynic, the Rat and the Fist](/wiki/The_Cynic,_the_Rat_and_the_Fist "The Cynic, the Rat and the Fist")* (1977). Returning to the United States in 1985, Milian performed supporting roles in movies like *[JFK](/wiki/JFK_(film) "JFK (film)")* (1991), *[Amistad](/wiki/Amistad_(film) "Amistad (film)")* (1997), *[Traffic](/wiki/Traffic_(2000_film) "Traffic (2000 film)")* (2000), and *[The Lost City](/wiki/The_Lost_City_(2005_film) "The Lost City (2005 film)")* (2005), and the television series *[Oz](/wiki/Oz_(TV_series) "Oz (TV series)")* (1997).[[3]](#cite_note-Bio-3)

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tomas_Milian&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

Milián was born in the [Cotorro](/wiki/Cotorro "Cotorro") municipality of [Havana](/wiki/Havana "Havana") in 1933.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) His father, also named Tomás, was a General in the [Machado regime](/wiki/Gerardo_Machado "Gerardo Machado").[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) After the [Revolt of the Sergeants](/wiki/Revolt_of_the_Sergeants "Revolt of the Sergeants") and the start of the [Fulgencio Batista](/wiki/Fulgencio_Batista "Fulgencio Batista")'s dictatorship, the elder Tomás was arrested, jailed, and later committed suicide on New Year's Eve 1946 in the presence of his son.

In 1955, after seeing the film [*East of Eden*](/wiki/East_of_Eden_(film) "East of Eden (film)"), Milián decided to leave Cuba and pursue his wishes of being an actor.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4)[[5]](#cite_note-virg-5) He moved to the United States, enrolling in the [University of Miami](/wiki/University_of_Miami "University of Miami"). After two years, he moved to New York City to study [method acting](/wiki/Method_acting "Method acting") at the [Actors Studio](/wiki/Actors_Studio "Actors Studio"), as a student of [Lee Strasberg](/wiki/Lee_Strasberg "Lee Strasberg").[[6]](#cite_note-6) Around that time, he became an American citizen.

## Acting career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tomas_Milian&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Acting career")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Delfini-Milian.png/250px-Delfini-Milian.png)](/wiki/File:Delfini-Milian.png)

Milian in *[Silver Spoon Set](/wiki/Silver_Spoon_Set "Silver Spoon Set")* (1960)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Giorno_per_giorno%2C_disperatamente_-_Tomas_Milian.jpg/250px-Giorno_per_giorno%2C_disperatamente_-_Tomas_Milian.jpg)](/wiki/File:Giorno_per_giorno,_disperatamente_-_Tomas_Milian.jpg)

Milian in *[Day by Day, Desperately](/wiki/Day_by_Day,_Desperately "Day by Day, Desperately")* (1961).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Tomas_Milian_Milano_odia.png/250px-Tomas_Milian_Milano_odia.png)](/wiki/File:Tomas_Milian_Milano_odia.png)

Milian in [*Almost Human*](/wiki/Almost_Human_(1974_film) "Almost Human (1974 film)") (1974).

After starting a career in the United States, Milian went to Italy in 1958 to take part in a theatre festival in [Spoleto](/wiki/Spoleto "Spoleto").[[7]](#cite_note-7) In Italy, he was discovered by director [Mauro Bolognini](/wiki/Mauro_Bolognini "Mauro Bolognini") and appeared in supporting roles in several drama films during the late 1950s and early 1960s.

He eventually decided to relocate to Italy, where he lived for over 25 years, becoming a very successful performer. His first film part in Italy was in the 1959 picture *[La notte brava](/wiki/La_notte_brava "La notte brava")*. Although his voice was usually [dubbed](/wiki/Dubbing_(filmmaking) "Dubbing (filmmaking)") due to his accent, Milián performed his lines in Italian (or in English, depending on the film). He initially starred in [arthouse](/wiki/Arthouse "Arthouse") movies and worked with directors such as [Mauro Bolognini](/wiki/Mauro_Bolognini "Mauro Bolognini"), [Luchino Visconti](/wiki/Luchino_Visconti "Luchino Visconti"), and [Pier Paolo Pasolini](/wiki/Pier_Paolo_Pasolini "Pier Paolo Pasolini").[[5]](#cite_note-virg-5)

After five years of making what he deemed "intellectual" movies, Milián was unhappy with his contract with producer [Franco Cristaldi](/wiki/Franco_Cristaldi "Franco Cristaldi") and thought of going back to the United States. Needing money to start over, he took the opportunity to star as a bandit in a [Spaghetti Western](/wiki/Spaghetti_Western "Spaghetti Western") called *[The Bounty Killer](/wiki/The_Bounty_Killer "The Bounty Killer")*. The film boosted his career,[[8]](#cite_note-8) and ultimately resulted in his staying in Italy. He became a star of the Spaghetti Western genre,[[9]](#cite_note-9) where he often played Mexican bandits or revolutionaries, roles in which he spoke in his real voice. He starred in *[The Ugly Ones](/wiki/The_Ugly_Ones "The Ugly Ones")* (1966), *[The Big Gundown](/wiki/The_Big_Gundown "The Big Gundown")* (1966), *[Django Kill... If You Live, Shoot!](/wiki/Django_Kill..._If_You_Live,_Shoot! "Django Kill... If You Live, Shoot!")* (1967), *[Face to Face](/wiki/Face_to_Face_(1967_film) "Face to Face (1967 film)")* (1967), *[Run, Man, Run](/wiki/Run,_Man,_Run "Run, Man, Run")* (1968), *[Death Sentence](/wiki/Death_Sentence_(1968_film) "Death Sentence (1968 film)")* (1968), *[Tepepa](/wiki/Tepepa "Tepepa")* (1969), *[Compañeros](/wiki/Compa%C3%B1eros_(film) "Compañeros (film)")* (1970), *[Sonny and Jed](/wiki/Sonny_and_Jed "Sonny and Jed")* (1972), *[Life Is Tough, Eh Providence?](/wiki/Life_Is_Tough,_Eh_Providence%3F "Life Is Tough, Eh Providence?")* (1972) and *[Four of the Apocalypse](/wiki/Four_of_the_Apocalypse "Four of the Apocalypse")* (1975).

In 1969, he became a [naturalized](/wiki/Naturalized "Naturalized") Italian citizen.[[5]](#cite_note-virg-5)

As the Spaghetti Westerns dwindled, Milián remained a star in many genre films, playing both villains and heroes in various [polizieschi](/wiki/Polizieschi "Polizieschi") movies. He starred with [Barbara Bouchet](/wiki/Barbara_Bouchet "Barbara Bouchet") in the [giallo](/wiki/Giallo "Giallo") *[Don't Torture a Duckling](/wiki/Don%27t_Torture_a_Duckling "Don't Torture a Duckling")*.[[3]](#cite_note-Bio-3) In addition to his role in *[Almost Human](/wiki/Almost_Human_(1974_film) "Almost Human (1974 film)")* (1974) and appearances in *[Emergency Squad](/wiki/Emergency_Squad_(1974_film) "Emergency Squad (1974 film)")* (1974), *[The Tough Ones](/wiki/The_Tough_Ones_(1976_film) "The Tough Ones (1976 film)")* (1976) and *[The Cynic, the Rat and the Fist](/wiki/The_Cynic,_the_Rat_and_the_Fist "The Cynic, the Rat and the Fist")* (1977), he also appeared in two film series - [Bruno Corbucci](/wiki/Bruno_Corbucci "Bruno Corbucci")'s *Nico Giraldi* series (1976-1984, beginning with *[The Cop in Blue Jeans](/wiki/The_Cop_in_Blue_Jeans "The Cop in Blue Jeans")*) and [Umberto Lenzi](/wiki/Umberto_Lenzi "Umberto Lenzi")'s *Er Monnezza* films (1976-1980, beginning with *[Free Hand for a Tough Cop](/wiki/Free_Hand_for_a_Tough_Cop "Free Hand for a Tough Cop")*). His other films during this period include the giallo *[Don't Torture a Duckling](/wiki/Don%27t_Torture_a_Duckling "Don't Torture a Duckling")* (1972) and the non-genre films *[The Last Movie](/wiki/The_Last_Movie "The Last Movie")* (1971), *[Luna](/wiki/La_Luna_(1979_film) "La Luna (1979 film)")* (1979), *[Identification of a Woman](/wiki/Identification_of_a_Woman "Identification of a Woman")* (1982) and *[Monsignor](/wiki/Monsignor_(film) "Monsignor (film)")* (1982).

He later turned to comedy, playing the recurrent characters of petty thief Monnezza and [Serpico](/wiki/Frank_Serpico "Frank Serpico")-like police officer Nico Giraldi in a variety of crime-comedy pictures. Although his voice was dubbed most of the time by [Ferruccio Amendola](/wiki/Ferruccio_Amendola "Ferruccio Amendola"), Milián wrote his own lines in [Roman](/wiki/Rome "Rome") slang. Milián's inventive use of *[romanesco](/wiki/Romanesco_dialect "Romanesco dialect")* (Roman dialect) made him a cult performer in Italy. [Bruno Corbucci](/wiki/Bruno_Corbucci "Bruno Corbucci"), the director of many of these films commented, *"At the cinemas as soon as Tomás Milián appeared on the screen, when he made a wisecrack and in the heaviest situations, then it was a pandemonium, it was like being at the stadium."*[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] As Milián used similar makeups and accents in portraying both characters, Monnezza and Nico were occasionally confused by Italian audiences, who sometimes referred erroneously to them both as *Monnezza*, or *Er Monnezza* (*Da trash* in [Roman slang](/wiki/Romanesco_dialect "Romanesco dialect") ), and still closely associate Milián with these performances.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Milián also appeared in non-genre pictures, such as [Bernardo Bertolucci](/wiki/Bernardo_Bertolucci "Bernardo Bertolucci")'s *[La Luna](/wiki/La_Luna_(1979_film) "La Luna (1979 film)")*, for which he won a [Nastro d'Argento for Best supporting Actor](/wiki/Nastro_d%27Argento_Best_supporting_Actor "Nastro d'Argento Best supporting Actor"), and [Michelangelo Antonioni](/wiki/Michelangelo_Antonioni "Michelangelo Antonioni")'s *[Identification of a Woman](/wiki/Identification_of_a_Woman "Identification of a Woman")*.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

As he grew older, Milián decided to go back to the United States. He appeared in [Sydney Pollack](/wiki/Sydney_Pollack "Sydney Pollack")'s *[Havana](/wiki/Havana_(film) "Havana (film)")*, [Steven Spielberg](/wiki/Steven_Spielberg "Steven Spielberg")'s *[Amistad](/wiki/Amistad_(1997_film) "Amistad (1997 film)")*, [Steven Soderbergh](/wiki/Steven_Soderbergh "Steven Soderbergh")'s *[Traffic](/wiki/Traffic_(2000_film) "Traffic (2000 film)")* as well as [Andy García](/wiki/Andy_Garc%C3%ADa "Andy García")'s [*The Lost City*](/wiki/The_Lost_City_(2005_film) "The Lost City (2005 film)"), about Revolutionary Cuba. He has also played many roles on stage. In 2005, he portrayed Generalisimo [Rafael Leonidas Trujillo Molina](/wiki/Rafael_Leonidas_Trujillo_Molina "Rafael Leonidas Trujillo Molina") in the film version of [Mario Vargas Llosa](/wiki/Mario_Vargas_Llosa "Mario Vargas Llosa")'s novel *[The Feast of the Goat](/wiki/The_Feast_of_the_Goat "The Feast of the Goat")*.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Musical career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tomas_Milian&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Musical career")]

Milián, in addition to his acting career, was also an accomplished musician. In 1966, he founded the Tomas Milian Group with [Ray Lovelock](/wiki/Ray_Lovelock_(actor) "Ray Lovelock (actor)") (vocals), Maurizio Luzi (guitar), Aldo "Fido" Colangelo (keyboard, vocals), Mario Piccinno (drums), Romeo Piccinno (guitar, vocals), and Peppe Colella (bass).[[13]](#cite_note-:1-13) He also wrote and performed the theme songs to several of his films.[[13]](#cite_note-:1-13)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tomas_Milian&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Milian was openly [bisexual](/wiki/Bisexual "Bisexual").[[14]](#cite_note-14) In a 2014 interview, he stated the first person he came out to was [Claudia Cardinale](/wiki/Claudia_Cardinale "Claudia Cardinale"). He married actress Margherita "Rita" Valletti with whom he had a son, Tomaso.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tomas_Milian&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Death")]

Milian was found dead from a stroke at his home in Miami on March 22, 2017.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

On October 11, 2017, he received the Leone in Memoriam award at the 7º [Almería Western Film Festival](/wiki/Almer%C3%ADa_Western_Film_Festival "Almería Western Film Festival").[[18]](#cite_note-18) It was picked up by his friend [Luis Santeiro](/wiki/Luis_Santeiro "Luis Santeiro").[[19]](#cite_note-19)
